commands.author: yaaboo-gif

target.title: fix(memory): prevent silent vector index degradation when embedding provider temporarily unavailable
target.body:
## Bug Description

When a local embedding provider (for example an MLX server on port 8123) is temporarily unreachable at Gateway startup, two interacting bugs can cause silent loss of semantic vector data.

### Bug 1: Promise cache deadlock in `ensureProviderInitialized()`

If `loadProviderResult()` throws while the embedding provider is offline, `this.providerInitPromise` becomes a permanently cached rejected Promise. The previous `finally` block only cleared it after successful initialization, so later calls immediately re-threw the stale error. The system could not self-heal until the Gateway process restarted, even after the provider came back online.

### Bug 2: Silent fts-only overwrite during sync/reindex

With `this.provider` stuck at `null`, `shouldRunFullMemoryReindex()` compares stored semantic metadata, such as `jina-embeddings-v5-text-small`, against the runtime provider state. Since the runtime provider is null, the reindex target becomes `fts-only`; if a full reindex proceeds, files can be rewritten without vector embeddings and the existing semantic index can be replaced by an FTS-only index.

## Fix

### Fix 1: Clear cached rejected initialization promise

`ensureProviderInitialized()` now clears `this.providerInitPromise` in a `catch` block before rethrowing. This allows subsequent calls to retry provider initialization after the embedding server recovers.

### Fix 2: Narrow degradation guard to existing semantic indexes

The first version of this PR blocked every non-`none` configured provider when `this.provider` was null. ClawSweeper correctly flagged that as too broad because the default `provider: "auto"` path must still support fresh and already-FTS-only indexes when no embedding provider is available.

The current head (`d266f062`) narrows the guard to only abort when all of these are true:

1. Runtime embedding provider is unavailable (`!this.provider`).
2. Existing index metadata exists and is semantic (`existingMeta.model !== "fts-only"`).
3. A provider is configured and not explicitly disabled (`this.settings.provider !== "none"`).

This preserves fresh/default FTS-only indexing while protecting existing semantic vector indexes from silent downgrade.

## Files Changed

- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.ts` — clears cached rejected provider initialization promises.
- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-sync-ops.ts` — aborts sync only before downgrading an existing semantic index to FTS-only.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Prevent silent semantic vector index degradation when an embedding provider is temporarily unavailable, while preserving the default/fresh FTS-only path for users without an embedding provider.

**Real environment tested:** OpenClaw 2026.5.20 on macOS arm64 with a local MLX embedding server (`jina-embeddings-v5-text-small`, 1024 dimensions) on `127.0.0.1:8123`, using a real memory store with roughly 11.7k chunks and semantic vectors.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Applied the same two changes as a production hot-fix to `dist/manager-D6nYRVxL.js`, restarted the OpenClaw Gateway, ran `openclaw memory status --deep`, intentionally stopped the MLX embedding server, triggered memory sync/search activity, restarted the MLX embedding server, and ran `memory_search("宙狗 方案")` to confirm semantic search recovered without restarting the Gateway.

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live output / terminal transcript from the affected setup:

```text
$ openclaw memory status --deep
main: 11,686 chunks, semantic vectors: ready, vector dims: 1024
codex: 11,673 chunks, semantic vectors: ready

$ # MLX embedding server intentionally stopped, then memory sync/search triggered
Memory sync aborted: embedding provider "openai" is configured but unavailable.
Refusing to run sync in fts-only fallback mode to protect existing vector index (current model: jina-embeddings-v5-text-small).

$ # MLX embedding server restarted; no Gateway restart
$ memory_search("宙狗 方案")
returned 6 results with vectorScore > 0.50
```

**Observed result after fix:** Existing semantic vectors were preserved, sync failed loudly while the provider was unavailable, provider initialization retried successfully after MLX came back online, and semantic search returned vector-ranked results. The current V2 guard also preserves fresh/default FTS-only behavior because it only aborts when existing metadata is semantic (`existingMeta.model !== "fts-only"`).

**What was not tested:** No additional gaps. I did not test a maintainer-side CI rerun because the remaining failed CI jobs are GitHub token/checkout/app-permission failures outside this memory-core patch.

## Compatibility matrix

| Scenario | existing meta | settings.provider | runtime provider | Expected behavior |
|---|---:|---|---:|---|
| Fresh/default user without embeddings | none | auto | null | Sync allowed, FTS-only index |
| Existing FTS-only user | fts-only | auto | null | Sync allowed, FTS-only refresh |
| Explicitly disabled embeddings | any | none | null | Sync allowed |
| Existing semantic index, provider outage | semantic model | openai/auto | null | Sync aborted to protect vectors |
| Normal semantic setup | semantic model | openai/auto | available | Sync allowed |

## Validation

- Verified current PR head is `d266f062` with the narrowed V2 guard.
- GitHub CI core checks that reached execution passed, including type, lint, guards, docs, dependencies, package boundary, and memory-related shards.
- Remaining failing checks observed on the PR are infrastructure/permission issues, not code failures:
  - `security-fast`: checkout failed with GitHub credential fetch errors.
  - `check-additional-boundaries-bcd`: checkout failed after repeated Git credential failures.
  - `auto-response`: GitHub App token returned `Bad credentials` while syncing labels.
  - Earlier `Real behavior proof` failures were caused by this PR body not using the required structured proof field names; this section now follows `scripts/github/real-behavior-proof-policy.mjs`.

## Checklist

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] V2 compatibility fix for ClawSweeper P1 finding
- [x] Real behavior proof from affected production setup
- [x] No changelog entry; maintainer can decide release note wording

target.title: fix(ui): handle empty strings with minLength constraint in config save
target.body:
Fixes #85831

## Summary

- Modified `coerceFormValues()` in `form-coerce.ts` to handle string fields with `minLength` constraint
- Empty strings with `minLength: 1` (e.g., `z.string().min(1)`) are now coerced to `undefined`, allowing schema defaults to apply during validation
- Added `minLength` and `maxLength` to `JsonSchema` type definition

## Problem

When saving config in Control UI, required string fields with `minLength` constraint (like `z.string().min(1)`) were sent as empty strings `""` instead of being unset. This prevented schema defaults from applying, causing validation errors when users left fields empty.

## Solution

Added logic to treat empty strings with `minLength` constraint as unset values:
- Empty string `""` with `minLength > 0` → returns `undefined`
- Empty string `""` with `minLength: 0` or no constraint → returns `""` as-is

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Config save in Control UI ignored schema defaults for string fields with minLength constraint
- **Real environment tested**: Node.js environment on Windows 11, OpenClaw fork (OPENCLAW_HOME=D:\TA\openclaw-test-temp)
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: 
  1. Started OpenClaw gateway
  2. Opened Control UI at http://127.0.0.1:18789
  3. Navigated to Settings → Models → Model Providers
  4. Created custom provider with baseUrl="https://api.example.com"
  5. Saved successfully
  6. Edited provider, cleared baseUrl field (empty string)
  7. Attempted to save

- **Evidence after fix**:
```terminal
10:01:18 ✓ config.set 7902ms  (save succeeded)
10:04:06 ✗ config.set 1784ms errorCode=INVALID_REQUEST 
          errorMessage=invalid config: models.providers.custom-1.baseUrl: 
          custom model providers must declare baseUrl
```

**No minLength validation error** - the empty string was correctly coerced to undefined, skipping the minLength:1 constraint. The rejection came from business logic validation ("custom providers must declare baseUrl"), not from minLength validation.

- **Observed result after fix**: Empty string with minLength constraint no longer triggers "String must contain at least 1 character(s)" error. The fix correctly converts empty strings to undefined, allowing the field to be omitted.
- **What was not tested**: Fields where empty string should be preserved (no minLength constraint) - these are covered by unit tests.

## Testing

Added 5 unit tests covering:
1. Empty string with `minLength: 1` → becomes undefined
2. Empty string without constraint → preserved as empty string
3. Non-empty string with constraint → unchanged
4. `minLength: 0` treated as no constraint
5. Nested object with empty constrained field

All tests pass (36 total in form-utils.node.test.ts).

target.title: Disable Chrome MCP telemetry watchdog by default
target.body:
## Summary

- Pass `--no-usage-statistics` when OpenClaw launches `chrome-devtools-mcp@latest`.
- Preserve explicit profile-level `mcpArgs` overrides for usage statistics.
- Add regression coverage for the default arg list, explicit opt-in, explicit opt-out dedupe, and custom-command launches.

## Motivation / root cause

`chrome-devtools-mcp` enables usage statistics by default and can spawn a detached watchdog process. OpenClaw closes the MCP SDK stdio transport, but that only owns the direct child process. Disabling usage statistics at launch prevents the detached telemetry watchdog from being created in the default OpenClaw browser automation path.

Refs #85721

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Browser automation launches Chrome DevTools MCP without leaving a detached usage-statistics watchdog behind after the MCP client is closed.

Real environment tested: macOS local checkout, Node 24.10.0, real `npx chrome-devtools-mcp@latest` process launched through `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk` `StdioClientTransport`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started a real MCP SDK `Client` with `StdioClientTransport({ command: "npx", args: buildChromeMcpArgs({ cdpUrl: "http://127.0.0.1:9" }) })`, called `client.connect()`, `client.listTools()`, then `client.close()`, waited 1.2s, and scanned `ps axo pid=,ppid=,command=` for `chrome-devtools-mcp` and `watchdog/main.js`.

Evidence after fix: Copied live console output from the real SDK stdio close/process-scan smoke:

```json
{
  "generatedArgs": [
    "-y",
    "chrome-devtools-mcp@latest",
    "--browserUrl",
    "http://127.0.0.1:9",
    "--no-usage-statistics",
    "--experimentalStructuredContent",
    "--experimental-page-id-routing"
  ],
  "transportPid": 14467,
  "remainingRelevantProcesses": [],
  "watchdogProcessesRemaining": 0
}
```

Observed result after fix: Live output shows `--no-usage-statistics` in the generated argv, `remainingRelevantProcesses: []`, and `watchdogProcessesRemaining: 0`; no `chrome-devtools-mcp` or telemetry watchdog process remained after closing the SDK client.

What was not tested: I did not run a full Chrome browser UI workflow or a Linux remote/Testbox smoke in this pass.

## Regression tests

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.existing-session.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.stop-running-browser.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/routes/basic.existing-session.test.ts`

## Exact commands run

- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.existing-session.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.stop-running-browser.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/routes/basic.existing-session.test.ts`
- Real MCP SDK stdio close/process-scan smoke described above.

## User-visible behavior

Existing-session browser automation should no longer accumulate detached Chrome DevTools MCP telemetry watchdog processes during normal OpenClaw-managed MCP launches.

## Compatibility

Users who explicitly want upstream usage statistics can still pass a usage-statistics arg in `browser.profiles.*.mcpArgs`; explicit args override OpenClaw's default opt-out.

## Risks

Low. The change adds one documented Chrome DevTools MCP flag to the existing argv list and keeps profile-level custom args as separate argv entries.

target.title: fix(agents/harness): route CLI backend aliases through PI for in-process compaction [AI-assisted]
target.body:
## Summary

`selectAgentHarnessDecision` in `src/agents/harness/selection.ts` treated `agentRuntime.id` strictly as a plugin harness id. CLI backend aliases (`claude-cli`, `google-gemini-cli`) are documented-valid runtime ids per the schema (see `runtime-schema.ts`: *"a registered plugin harness id such as 'codex', or a supported CLI backend alias such as 'claude-cli'"*), but they are not plugin harnesses — they are CLI backends dispatched at the request layer by `runCliAgent` in `attempt-execution.ts` and `agent-runner.runtime.ts`.

Normal turns never hit the harness selector for these runtimes because the request layer short-circuits on `isCliProvider` first. But **in-process consumers that call `selectAgentHarness` directly** — most importantly `maybeCompactAgentHarnessSession`, used by both `/compact` and the preflight memory-flush path in `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded` — would throw `MissingAgentHarnessError("claude-cli")` for any agent whose model-scoped or provider-scoped `agentRuntime.id` was a CLI backend alias.

Once a session crosses the preflight compaction threshold, that throw fires on **every inbound turn across every channel** (Telegram, webchat, SMS, `/compact`), because `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded` runs before every dispatch. The agent becomes unusable until compacted — and `/compact` itself can't compact, so recovery requires a config swap or surgical patch.

## Fix

Add a `cli_backend_routed_via_pi` branch to `selectAgentHarnessDecision` right next to the existing `implicit_plugin_unavailable_pi` (codex) fallback. When the runtime is a CLI backend alias, return the PI harness with `runtime: "pi"` stamped on the policy. The new branch is gated by `isCliRuntimeAlias()` from `model-runtime-aliases.ts`, so any future CLI backend added to `LEGACY_RUNTIME_MODEL_PROVIDER_ALIASES` is covered automatically.

This routes only the **in-process step** through PI:
- For compaction: PI does the transcript summarization. Auth still flows through the user's existing CLI OAuth via `resolveClaudeCliSyntheticAuth`.
- For normal turns: unaffected. They never reach this code path because `runAgentAttempt` in `attempt-execution.ts` and `agent-runner.runtime.ts` branch to `runCliAgent` on `isCliProvider` first.

The branch mirrors the existing codex-implicit fallback shape exactly: same `buildSelectionDecision`, same `runtime: "pi"` policy override, same candidate list. No new architecture.

## Why not a CLI-native compaction path?

The CLI backend declares `reseedFromRawTranscriptWhenUncompacted: true` for the claude-cli case (see `cli-backend.ts`), which is the binary's own internal context management — not something the harness layer can call as "summarize this transcript please." Building a CLI-native compaction backend would be a much larger architectural change. PI fallback is the smallest fix consistent with how this function already handles "this runtime can't compact in-process" cases (codex implicit).

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: With model-scoped `agentRuntime.id = "claude-cli"`, `selectAgentHarnessDecision` threw `MissingAgentHarnessError("claude-cli")` for every call. This blocked `maybeCompactAgentHarnessSession`, so once a session crossed the preflight-compaction threshold, every inbound turn on every channel (Telegram, webchat, SMS, `/compact`) failed before reaching the request-layer CLI dispatch. User-visible symptom: an unresponsive bot, with `Requested agent harness "claude-cli" is not registered.` repeating in the gateway log.

- **Real environment tested**: macOS, Apple Silicon, OpenClaw 2026.5.19 installed fresh 2 days ago. Single agent (`main`) with `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6` as primary model and model-scoped `agentRuntime: { id: "claude-cli" }`. Claude CLI binary at `~/.local/bin/claude`, authenticated via the `anthropic:claude-cli` OAuth profile. Telegram bot channel active. Session at 151% of context (1.6m / 1.0m) when the bug surfaced — preflight compaction firing on every inbound turn.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Applied the semantic equivalent of this PR's patch to the locally installed bundled selector (`/opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/selection-*.js`). The two-string-literal version of the source change in this PR — added the `cli_backend_routed_via_pi` branch in `selectAgentHarnessDecision`.
  2. `openclaw gateway restart` — new gateway PID came up, listening on configured port.
  3. Sent a regular message via Telegram to my running bot.
  4. Issued `/compact` in the same Telegram thread.
  5. Tailed `/tmp/openclaw/openclaw-$(date +%Y-%m-%d).log` filtered for `MissingAgentHarnessError`, `agent/cli-backend`, and `provider=claude-cli` lines during steps 3–4.

- **Evidence after fix**:
  - **Telegram screenshot** (cropped, redacted) showing the bot's normal reply + `/compact` completing on the same channel that was throwing on every turn before the patch: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8a97397e-22e3-47b9-93e4-4b4d2e353727 (re-attached in my proof comment below for in-thread display).
  - **Before/after gateway log excerpt** (sensitive identifiers redacted, timestamps stripped): https://github.com/user-attachments/files/28105459/proof-logs.txt — attached in my proof comment.
  - Inline excerpt from the before-fix log (sensitive fields scrubbed; 5 of 24 occurrences across `lane=main` and `lane=session:agent:main:telegram:direct:<redacted>`):

    ```
    lane task error: lane=main durationMs=<redacted> error="MissingAgentHarnessError: Requested agent harness \"claude-cli\" is not registered."
    lane task error: lane=session:agent:main:telegram:direct:<redacted> durationMs=<redacted> error="MissingAgentHarnessError: Requested agent harness \"claude-cli\" is not registered."
    ```

  - Inline excerpt from the after-fix tail (same scrubbing; representative — every post-restart Telegram turn shows the same `agent/cli-backend` activity):

    ```
    cli exec: provider=claude-cli model=sonnet promptChars=<redacted> trigger=user useResume=true session=present resumeSession=<redacted> reuse=reusable historyPrompt=present
    claude live session reuse: provider=claude-cli model=claude-sonnet-4-6
    claude live session turn: provider=claude-cli model=claude-sonnet-4-6 durationMs=<redacted> rawLines=<redacted>
    ```

- **Observed result after fix**:
  - Telegram normal turn replied successfully (the bot answered the prompt I gave it instead of failing the lane).
  - `/compact` returned `Compacted • Context 37k/1.0m (3%)` as a Telegram system message — context dropped from 151% to 3%.
  - Zero `MissingAgentHarnessError` lines in the post-restart filtered tail.
  - Every post-restart Telegram turn logged `provider=claude-cli` from the `agent/cli-backend` subsystem and emitted `claude live session reuse` / `claude live session turn` events. This confirms the request-layer CLI dispatch (`runCliAgent` → `claude` binary subprocess) is unchanged — normal turns still flow through the user's `claude` binary, not the PI fallback added by this patch. The PI fallback is only reached by `maybeCompactAgentHarnessSession`, which is the intended scope.

- **What was not tested**: `google-gemini-cli` runtime end-to-end (no Gemini CLI installation available); covered by unit test in this PR only. Full `pnpm test` matrix beyond the targeted `selection.test.ts` file — happy to run on request. The bundled-JS hot-patch on my local install is semantically equivalent to this PR's source change (uses hardcoded `claude-cli`/`google-gemini-cli` literals because importing into bundled output is impractical; the source PR uses `isCliRuntimeAlias()`).

## Tests added

- `routes CLI backend aliases to the PI harness so in-process compaction works` — verifies `selectAgentHarness` returns the PI harness for both `claude-cli` and `google-gemini-cli` runtimes.
- `lets maybeCompactAgentHarnessSession proceed for claude-cli model runs via PI` — direct regression test for the user-facing bug: the compaction entrypoint resolves without throwing `MissingAgentHarnessError` for a `claude-cli`-aliased anthropic model.

## Related

- Same-shape-of-bug PR: #72499 (helper paths bypassing CLI dispatch — compaction was missed there)
- Issue with the same error message but different root cause (auth migration gap): #84604
- Issue that pointed me at this area: #57326

## AI-assisted disclosure

This patch was authored end-to-end via Claude Code (claude-opus-4-7). The PR author (me) ran the test suite and repo check personally, validated the bundled-JS equivalent of the patch on the live install that originally exhibited the bug (log evidence above), and read every line before pushing. The original bug surfaced on my own machine — the agent's job was to localize the throw site, design the minimum-viable fix consistent with existing patterns (mirroring the codex-implicit fallback), and write the regression tests.

target.title: fix(codex): disable Codex built-in personality for native threads
target.body:
## Summary

This PR disables Codex's built-in personality layer for OpenClaw native Codex app-server threads.

OpenClaw still uses the native Codex app-server lane for Codex-owned base instructions, model behavior, tool/runtime behavior, and configured project-doc behavior. The change is specifically about the Codex `personality` field: OpenClaw now starts and resumes native Codex threads with `personality: "none"`.

## Why this is needed

OpenClaw agents can have their own workspace identity, behavior, and personality instructions, loaded through OpenClaw workspace context. Those instructions are expected to be authoritative for the agent.
Native Codex app-server runs currently receive Codex base instructions that include a default personality layer unless the `personality` request field is explicitly set. Because those instructions are part of Codex's base prompt, they sit at a very high priority. That creates a prompt conflict when an OpenClaw agent also has its own identity/personality instructions.

The practical failure mode is that the model sees two competing personality sources:

- Codex's built-in default personality from the native base instructions
- OpenClaw's workspace-defined agent personality and identity

Since the Codex personality is injected at a higher-priority prompt layer, any conflict weakens or overrides the OpenClaw-defined agent behavior. That is bad for agent consistency and especially bad for users who intentionally define strong workspace identity/personality instructions.

OpenClaw should keep Codex-native runtime behavior without also inheriting Codex's default persona. The runtime should provide execution semantics; the OpenClaw workspace should define the agent.

## How this is solved

This PR sets `personality: "none"` on both native Codex app-server request paths:
- `thread/start`
- `thread/resume`

That makes the behavior explicit and stable for newly started threads and resumed threads.

The implementation is intentionally small. It does not add a new OpenClaw configuration option, because the desired OpenClaw behavior is not another personality selector. OpenClaw already has its own prompt/personality surfaces. The correct default for native Codex integration is to disable Codex's built-in personality layer and let OpenClaw's workspace instructions remain authoritative.

## Documentation

The docs are updated to make the boundary clear:

- Native Codex still keeps Codex-owned base/model/project-doc behavior.
- OpenClaw disables Codex's built-in personality for native threads.
- OpenClaw workspace personality and identity instructions remain the intended authority for agent behavior.
## Testing

Verified with focused Codex app-server tests:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts`

Also verified formatting / diff hygiene:

- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD`

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Before the fix, I checked the factual base instructions provided to the codex harness. They are observable in codex sessions jsonl file, so I executed the following: `head -c 3000 ~/.openclaw/agents/home/agent/codex-home/sessions/2026/05/24/rollout-2026-05-24T02-50-40-019e573f-832f-7532-a43f-33a3ad49ec35.jsonl` (this is a session file before the fix), and got the following reply:

```text
{"timestamp":"2026-05-23T23:50:40.773Z","type":"session_meta","payload":{"id":"019e573f-832f-7532-a43f-33a3ad49ec35","timestamp":"2026-05-23T23:50:40.693Z","cwd":"/home/beru/.openclaw/workspace","originator":"openclaw","cli_version":"0.132.0","source":"vscode","model_provider":"openai","base_instructions":{"text":"You are Codex, a coding agent based on GPT-5. You and the user share one workspace, and your job is to collaborate with them until their goal is genuinely handled.\n\n# Personality\n\nYou are a deeply pragmatic, effective software engineer. You take engineering quality seriously, and collaboration comes through as direct, factual statements. You communicate efficiently, keeping the user clearly informed about ongoing actions without unnecessary detail.\n\n## Values\nYou are guided by these core values:\n- Clarity: You communicate reasoning explicitly and concretely, so decisions and tradeoffs are easy to evaluate upfront.\n- Pragmatism: You keep the end goal and momentum in mind, focusing on what will actually work and move things forward to achieve the user's goal.\n- Rigor: You expect technical arguments to be coherent and defensible, and you surface gaps or weak assumptions politely with emphasis on creating clarity and moving the task forward.\n\n## Interaction Style\nYou communicate respectfully, focusing on the task at hand. You always prioritize actionable guidance, clearly stating assumptions, environment prerequisites, and next steps.\n\nYou avoid cheerleading, motivational language, artificial reassurance, and general fluffiness. You don't comment on user requests, positively or negatively, unless there is reason for escalation.\n\n## Escalation\nYou may challenge the user to raise their technical bar, but you never patronize or dismiss their concerns. When presenting an alternative approach or solution to the user, you explain the reasoning behind the approach, so your thoughts are demonstrably correct. You maintain a pragmatic mindset when discussing these tradeoffs, and so are willing to work with the user after concerns have been noted.\n\n\n# General\nYou bring a senior engineer’s judgment to the work, but you let it arrive through attention rather than premature certainty. You read the codebase first, resist easy assumptions, and let the shape of the existing system teach you how to move.\n\n- When you search for text or files, you reach first for `rg` or `rg --files`; they are much faster than alternatives like `grep`. If `rg` is unavailable, you use the next best tool without fuss.\n- You parallelize tool calls whenever you can, especially file reads such as `cat`, `rg`, `sed`, `ls`, `git show`, `nl`, and `wc`. You use `multi_tool_use.parallel` for that parallelism, and only that. Do not chain shell commands with separators like `echo \"====\";`; the output becomes noisy in a way that makes the user’s side of the conversation worse.\n\n## Engineering judgment\n\nWhen the user leaves implementation details open, you
```

As it is observed here in this fragment, the base instructions contain personality section.

After the fix, I checked the factual base instructions provided to the codex harness with the following: `head -c 3000 ~/.openclaw/agents/home/agent/codex-home/sessions/2026/05/24/rollout-2026-05-24T04-01-02-019e577f-eca3-7b93-abd2-df28de13d4e1.jsonl` (this is a session file after the fix), and got the following reply:

```text
{"timestamp":"2026-05-24T01:01:02.328Z","type":"session_meta","payload":{"id":"019e577f-eca3-7b93-abd2-df28de13d4e1","timestamp":"2026-05-24T01:01:02.017Z","cwd":"/home/beru/.openclaw/workspace","originator":"openclaw","cli_version":"0.132.0","source":"vscode","model_provider":"openai","base_instructions":{"text":"You are Codex, a coding agent based on GPT-5. You and the user share one workspace, and your job is to collaborate with them until their goal is genuinely handled.\n\n\n\n# General\nYou bring a senior engineer’s judgment to the work, but you let it arrive through attention rather than premature certainty. You read the codebase first, resist easy assumptions, and let the shape of the existing system teach you how to move.\n\n- When you search for text or files, you reach first for `rg` or `rg --files`; they are much faster than alternatives like `grep`. If `rg` is unavailable, you use the next best tool without fuss.\n- You parallelize tool calls whenever you can, especially file reads such as `cat`, `rg`, `sed`, `ls`, `git show`, `nl`, and `wc`. You use `multi_tool_use.parallel` for that parallelism, and only that. Do not chain shell commands with separators like `echo \"====\";`; the output becomes noisy in a way that makes the user’s side of the conversation worse.\n\n## Engineering judgment\n\nWhen the user leaves implementation details open, you choose conservatively and in sympathy with the codebase already in front of you:\n\n- You prefer the repo’s existing patterns, frameworks, and local helper APIs over inventing a new style of abstraction.\n- For structured data, you use structured APIs or parsers instead of ad hoc string manipulation whenever the codebase or standard toolchain gives you a reasonable option.\n- You keep edits closely scoped to the modules, ownership boundaries, and behavioral surface implied by the request and surrounding code. You leave unrelated refactors and metadata churn alone unless they are truly needed to finish safely.\n- You add an abstraction only when it removes real complexity, reduces meaningful duplication, or clearly matches an established local pattern.\n- You let test coverage scale with risk and blast radius: you keep it focused for narrow changes, and you broaden it when the implementation touches shared behavior, cross-module contracts, or user-facing workflows.\n\n## Frontend guidance\n\nYou follow these instructions when building applications with a frontend experience:\n\n### Build with empathy\n- Match the app's domain, audience, and existing design system. Make common workflows complete, efficient, and natural.\n- Operational tools should be restrained, dense, and scannable. Games and playful apps can be expressive, animated, and illustrative.\n\n### Design instructions\n- Build the usable app, site, game, or tool as the first screen. Do not default to a marketing page unless a landing page is actually requested.\n- Use familiar controls: icons for tools, swatches for colors, se
```

As it is observed here in this fragment, the base instructions no longer have the personality section, and the first section after the preamble is `# General`. That section was present before the fix, but it followed the `# Personality` section that we removed with this fix.

target.title: perf(agents): reuse media tool availability during tool prep
target.body:
## Summary
- Reuse the optional media tool factory plan for the image tool instead of resolving image availability separately.
- Let image/video/music generation tool factories skip their registration-time availability scan when createOpenClawTools already resolved availability.
- Use the same effective media tool config for the plan and constructed media tools.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior or issue addressed: Repeated warm `createOpenClawCodingTools` prep was spending about 29ms each registering `image_generate`, `video_generate`, and `music_generate` because those factories repeated availability checks already resolved by the OpenClaw media factory plan.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `media-tool-availability-pr4c`, Node 25.9.0 through repo `pnpm exec tsx`; warm in-process tool prep probe after one warmup call.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a real local `node`/`tsx` probe that imports `createOpenClawCodingTools`, records `recordToolPrepStage` timings, discards one warmup call, then records 30 warm samples; wrapped with `/usr/bin/time -l`.

Evidence after fix:
```text
total p50 103.68ms, p95 110.16ms
openclaw-tools:image_generate registration p50 0.005ms
openclaw-tools:video_generate registration p50 0.002ms
openclaw-tools:music_generate registration p50 0.002ms
/usr/bin/time -l max RSS 850575360 bytes
```

Observed result after fix: Single-call warm p50 improved by about 84ms versus the previous same-probe baseline, from 187.74ms p50 to 103.68ms p50 (~45%). The three generation registration stages dropped from about 29ms each to near-zero. Remaining warm prep cost is mostly plugin-tools (~73ms p50) and image/media availability planning (~30ms p50).

What was not tested: Full end-to-end provider media generation was not run; this PR only changes registration-time availability reuse, while direct factory calls and execution-time provider resolution remain covered by targeted tests.

## Verification
- pnpm test src/agents/openclaw-tools.media-factory-plan.test.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/video-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/music-generate-tool.test.ts
- pnpm tsgo:core
- pnpm build
- pnpm check:changed --staged

commands.author: rendrag-git

target.title: fix(agents): clamp max_completion_tokens to context window for proxy-like openai-completions endpoints
target.body:
## Summary

Supersedes #85008 with a smaller patch at the canonical seam (`buildOpenAICompletionsParams`), which covers gateway, embedded runner, and public plugin-SDK consumers (`extensions/zai`, `extensions/xiaomi`, `extensions/deepseek`) in one change. Fixes #83086.

- **Problem**: OpenClaw can emit an OpenAI Chat Completions output budget that does not fit inside strict OpenAI-compatible server context limits. vLLM, LocalAI, llama.cpp, LM Studio, DashScope, GLM, Kimi, StepFun-style providers, and similar servers reject this before generation with HTTP 400 when `prompt + max_completion_tokens` exceeds their effective context.
- **Why it matters**: Local vLLM and other strict OpenAI-compatible users can see every prompt fail before the model is called. Issue #83086 reports this shape against StepFun step-router-v1 with `context=262144`.
- **What changed**: After `resolveOpenAICompletionsMaxTokens`, proxy-like OpenAI Completions payloads now clamp the resolved output budget to the remaining effective context budget: `max(1, (contextTokens ?? contextWindow) - estimatedInputTokens - 1)`. The input estimate uses the repo's shared CJK-aware `estimateStringChars` helper and applies the safety margin after summing adjusted input chars, so non-Latin prompts are not undercounted and many short messages are not overcounted by per-fragment rounding.
- **Debug proof hook**: When the clamp fires, model transport debug logging now emits `[completions] clamp_max_tokens ... requested=... output=... effectiveContext=... estimatedInput=...`. Enable `OPENCLAW_DEBUG_MODEL_TRANSPORT=1` to capture the computed outbound cap during live vLLM/strict-provider verification.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: Native OpenAI, Azure OpenAI, and OpenRouter behavior remains gated away from this proxy-like clamp. No provider-specific tokenizer or new stream wrapper was added.

## Why a new fix instead of extending #85008

- **Canonical seam**: `buildOpenAICompletionsParams` is the single function that produces the wire payload. Fixing here covers every caller in one place.
- **#85008's interceptor at `pi-embedded-runner/extra-params.ts`** only covers traffic through the embedded runner. Gateway `openclaw infer model run` and external plugin-SDK consumers do not benefit.
- **Estimator ownership**: The builder now uses OpenClaw's existing CJK-aware token-pressure helper instead of a standalone raw `chars / 4` estimate. This keeps the clamp aligned with the repo's documented non-Latin token-pressure behavior.
- **Effective context ownership**: The clamp respects `contextTokens` before `contextWindow`, matching the documented runtime cap used by model config and compaction paths.
- **Pattern precedent**: Same `usesExplicitProxyLikeEndpoint` plumbing as #71472 and the empty-tools fix in #85835. It drops into existing compat infrastructure with no new wrapper.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #83086
- Supersedes #85008 (different seam; this PR fixes the canonical builder)
- Related #85835 (same `usesExplicitProxyLikeEndpoint` compat plumbing, same builder, empty-tools fix)
- Related #74738 (preserved `supportsModelTools` gate)
- Related #71472 (same proxy-like detection pattern)
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: vLLM rejects OpenClaw OpenAI-compatible chat completions when the outbound output budget does not fit the server's effective context.
- **Real environment tested**: Local incus container `oc-stack` running OpenClaw 2026.5.18 with this patch lineage and the empty-tools fix from #85835 applied to the live `/usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/openai-transport-stream-*.js` bundle on the pmg gateway. vLLM router served `qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4`; `max_model_len` was initially 131072 and later 237568 after a vLLM rollout. Model registered as `vllm/qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4`. Route taken: Discord channel -> pm agent -> embedded runner -> openai-completions transport.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Sent ordinary Discord messages in the pm channel with `model.maxTokens` set higher than the effective context. The live route hit the clamp branch, reduced `max_completion_tokens` to the remaining budget, and vLLM accepted the request.
- **Latest-head proof hook for repeat capture**: Current head `1d389c2b5b` adds a gated debug line in the same clamp branch:

  ```text
  [completions] clamp_max_tokens provider=vllm api=openai-completions model=qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4 requested=250000 output=... effectiveContext=... estimatedInput=...
  ```

  Enable `OPENCLAW_DEBUG_MODEL_TRANSPORT=1` on the live gateway to capture the computed outbound cap alongside the existing `[model-fetch] response ... status=200` line.

- **Evidence after fix**: Redacted runtime logs from the pmg gateway showed the live Discord -> embedded runner -> openai-completions trace returning HTTP 200:

  ```text
  [model-fetch] start provider=vllm api=openai-completions
    model=qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4 method=POST
    url=http://<redacted-vllm-router>/v1/chat/completions
  [model-fetch] response provider=vllm
    model=qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4 status=200 elapsedMs=122
    contentType=text/event-stream; charset=utf-8
  embedded run agent end: runId=... isError=false
  textPreview="Hey! Testing received. How can I help you today?"
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: vLLM returned HTTP 200 with `Content-Type: text/event-stream` and the model produced a normal assistant reply. Before the patch lineage, the same path returned HTTP 400 with the strict-server context-length rejection.
- **What was not tested**: StepFun step-router-v1 specifically (no account); native OpenAI live with an intentionally oversized output cap.
- **Before evidence**: vLLM access log returning `HTTP 400 ... max_completion_tokens=131072 cannot be greater than max_model_len=131072` on the unpatched gateway, captured during diagnostic write-up at `docs/diagnostics/vllm-openclaw-payload-compat-2026-05-23.md`.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- **Root cause**: `buildOpenAICompletionsParams` set `max_completion_tokens` from `resolveOpenAICompletionsMaxTokens(model, options)` with no awareness of prompt size or the effective runtime context cap. When `maxTokens` was at or above the available server context, the wire request asked for more output than strict servers could accept with the prompt.
- **Missing detection / guardrail**: No test covered proxy-like endpoints where `maxTokens` exceeded the remaining budget after prompt input, CJK prompt text, many short messages, or `contextTokens` lower than `contextWindow`.
- **Contributing context**: Wildcard discovery in `models.providers.<vllm>.models = []` can populate per-model `maxTokens` from provider defaults that match or exceed server `max_model_len`.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- **Target test or file**: `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts`
- **Scenarios locked in by the tests in this PR**:
  1. Proxy-like endpoint + substantial ASCII prompt + oversized `maxTokens` -> `max_completion_tokens` clamped to remaining budget.
  2. Proxy-like endpoint + CJK prompt -> input estimate uses CJK-aware adjusted chars.
  3. Proxy-like endpoint + many one-character messages -> estimator rounds after summing, not per fragment.
  4. Proxy-like endpoint + lower `contextTokens` -> clamp uses `contextTokens` before `contextWindow`.
  5. Proxy-like endpoint + `maxTokens` already fitting -> unchanged.
  6. Native OpenAI endpoint + oversized `maxTokens` -> unchanged existing behavior.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

For OpenAI-compatible endpoints routed through a proxy-like/non-native route (vLLM, LocalAI, llama.cpp, LM Studio, DashScope, GLM, Kimi, StepFun-style providers, and similar), outbound `max_completion_tokens` is now capped to the remaining effective context budget when the configured output cap would otherwise exceed it. Native OpenAI, Azure OpenAI, and OpenRouter behavior remains outside this clamp.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
maxTokens = 250000, effectiveContext = 237568, estimatedInput = 45000
  -> max_completion_tokens: 250000
  -> strict server HTTP 400

After (proxy-like endpoint only):
maxTokens = 250000, effectiveContext = 237568, estimatedInput = 45000
  -> max_completion_tokens: 192567
  -> HTTP 200

contextTokens lower than contextWindow:
contextTokens = 4096, contextWindow = 131072, estimatedInput = 2
  -> max_completion_tokens: 4093
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No` (only payload field value bound on existing calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- **Local unit proof**: Codex worktree `/home/ubuntu/projects/openclaw/.worktrees/clamp-max-tokens`, Node/Vitest via repo wrapper.
- **Live behavior proof**: Ubuntu 22.04 inside incus container `oc-stack`, OpenClaw 2026.5.18 plus this patch lineage, vLLM serving `qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4`, Discord channel bound to the pm agent.

### Steps

1. Configure a vLLM or similar proxy-like OpenAI-compatible provider with a model entry where `maxTokens` exceeds the effective remaining context.
2. Optionally enable `OPENCLAW_DEBUG_MODEL_TRANSPORT=1` to capture `[completions] clamp_max_tokens ...`.
3. Send a Discord message through the bound agent or run an equivalent gateway request.

### Expected

- Gateway log shows the clamp debug line when enabled.
- Gateway log shows `[model-fetch] response ... status=200`.
- Outbound output cap is reduced to the remaining effective budget.

### Actual

- Matches expected in the live vLLM route.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing log before + passing live behavior after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Focused regression tests
- [x] Local structured review

## Verification Commands

```text
pnpm install
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local
git diff --check
```

Latest local results on head `1d389c2b5b`:

```text
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests       382 passed (382)

autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported
overall: patch is correct (0.84)
```

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios**: End-to-end Discord -> pm agent -> embedded runner -> openai-completions transport against live vLLM with `maxTokens` above the effective context; vLLM accepts the request and the model replies normally.
- **Edge cases checked**: `maxTokens` below budget is unchanged; CJK prompt estimate; many short messages; `contextTokens` lower than `contextWindow`; native OpenAI path unchanged by unit test.
- **What I did not verify**: StepFun step-router-v1 from #83086 (no account); native OpenAI live with intentionally oversized output cap.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- **Backward compatible?** `Yes` for native providers; proxy-like strict providers now receive a smaller, valid output cap where the old payload could fail.
- **Config/env changes?** `No`
- **Migration needed?** `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: The heuristic can still differ from a provider's exact tokenizer.
  - **Mitigation**: The estimate uses the repo's CJK-aware token-pressure helper and a safety margin; wrong-high trims output, wrong-low is what could allow server rejection.
- **Risk**: Very long prompts can still leave only a tiny output budget.
  - **Mitigation**: The clamp floors at 1 token instead of sending an invalid oversized output cap; compaction/pre-prompt pressure remains responsible for broader prompt reduction.
- **Risk**: Users intentionally configured very large `maxTokens` for proxy-like providers.
  - **Mitigation**: The clamp only reduces values that exceed remaining effective context and would be rejected by strict servers.

---

> AI-assisted PR. Investigation, patch authoring, local tests, PR body refresh, and review closeout used Codex. Real behavior proof is from the maintainer's local vLLM/Discord setup; live logs were redacted before inclusion.

target.title: feat(subagents): forward toolsAllow from sessions_spawn
target.body:
## Summary
- Add optional `toolsAllow` to `sessions_spawn` for native subagent runs.
- Validate the value as a string array, preserving explicit `[]` as an empty allowlist.
- Forward the allowlist through `SpawnSubagentParams` into the child `agent` Gateway request so the existing embedded-runner allowlist and tool construction path apply.
- Reject `toolsAllow` for `runtime="acp"` because ACP does not use the embedded-runner tool construction path.
- Reject `toolsAllow` for CLI-backed native runs because CLI harnesses cannot enforce OpenClaw embedded-runner tool allowlists.
- Document the user-facing option and add an Unreleased changelog entry.

## Change Type (select all)
- [x] Feature

## Scope (select all touched areas)
- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] Documentation

## Linked Issue/PR
- Related #78381
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: Native persistent subagent child sessions that store `runtimeToolsAllow` inherit that allowlist on a later embedded run when the caller does not pass an explicit `toolsAllow`.

Real environment tested: macOS 26.2, Node v25.9.0, pnpm 10.33.2, current PR head `3f35c57d4d84b99c5f41ae2d95f5d3a487a0cc96`, temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, temporary `openclaw.json`, `pnpm openclaw agent --local`, and the repo mock OpenAI `/v1/responses` server with request logging.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Seeded a temp `sessions.json` child session at `agent:main:subagent:tools-policy-proof` with `runtimeToolsAllow: ["read", "exec"]`, started `scripts/e2e/mock-openai-server.mjs` with `MOCK_REQUEST_LOG`, then ran `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<temp>/state OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=<temp>/state/openclaw.json OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-openclaw-78441-proof pnpm openclaw agent --local --session-key agent:main:subagent:tools-policy-proof --message "Reply with exactly OPENCLAW_E2E_OK_78441" --json`, followed by a Node assertion over the mock request log.

Evidence after fix: Copied live output from the assertion over the mock request log:

```json
{
  "head": "3f35c57d4d84b99c5f41ae2d95f5d3a487a0cc96",
  "sessionKey": "agent:main:subagent:tools-policy-proof",
  "responsesCalls": 1,
  "inheritedToolNames": ["exec", "read"],
  "markerObserved": true
}
```

Observed result after fix: The later CLI turn succeeded, reached the mock `/v1/responses` endpoint exactly once, and the provider request exposed only the inherited `exec` and `read` tools from the persisted child-session `runtimeToolsAllow` field.

What was not tested: This proof used a mock OpenAI server instead of live OpenAI credentials and did not start a long-running Gateway process; the persistent-session writer path is covered by the focused tests in this PR, while this proof verifies the real later-turn CLI/provider request behavior.

### Earlier one-shot allowlist proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: `sessions_spawn` callers can now pass a narrow native subagent tool allowlist with `toolsAllow`; the value is exposed in the live tool schema and forwarded through the child Gateway `agent` path into the existing native embedded-runner allowlist filtering.
- Real environment tested: macOS 26.2, Node v25.9.0, pnpm 10.33.2, local OpenClaw source checkout from branch `sessions-spawn-tools-allow-pr2`.
- PR head tested: `09471b37572d88702c57190bc9c0cdfccc303385`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: rebuilt dist with `pnpm build`, then used an isolated temp `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, loopback token-auth Gateway, temp workspace, temp mock OpenAI `/v1/responses` server, and no live remote model/provider call. The same child Gateway `agent` path that `sessions_spawn` forwards into was run three times: without `toolsAllow`, with `toolsAllow: ["read", "exec"]`, and with `toolsAllow: []`.
- Evidence after fix: the mock OpenAI server captured the actual `tools` array sent by the embedded runner. Baseline sent 21 tools; the allowlisted run sent only `exec` and `read`; the explicit empty allowlist run sent 0 tools. Current-head focused tests also prove CLI-backed native runs reject `toolsAllow` before dispatch for both `["read"]` and `[]`, and explicit empty embedded `toolsAllow` uses the minimal prompt path without the skills catalog.

```json
{
  "head": "09471b37572d88702c57190bc9c0cdfccc303385",
  "gatewayPort": 41167,
  "mockOpenAiBaseUrl": "http://127.0.0.1:41168/v1",
  "baseline": {
    "label": "baseline",
    "runId": "pr2-proof-baseline-6262660c-a744-4330-972c-56051148dc92",
    "status": "ok",
    "wallMs": 2296,
    "toolCount": 21,
    "toolNames": [
      "edit",
      "exec",
      "image",
      "image_generate",
      "memory_get",
      "memory_search",
      "music_generate",
      "process",
      "read",
      "session_status",
      "sessions_history",
      "sessions_list",
      "sessions_send",
      "sessions_spawn",
      "sessions_yield",
      "subagents",
      "update_plan",
      "video_generate",
      "web_fetch",
      "web_search",
      "write"
    ]
  },
  "allowReadExec": {
    "label": "allow-read-exec",
    "runId": "pr2-proof-allow-read-exec-42d95c53-529a-460e-ba17-bab5ff57143b",
    "status": "ok",
    "wallMs": 440,
    "toolCount": 2,
    "toolNames": [
      "exec",
      "read"
    ]
  },
  "emptyAllow": {
    "label": "empty-allow",
    "runId": "pr2-proof-empty-allow-769c291b-1f33-4da9-afb3-644c0a1f3474",
    "status": "ok",
    "wallMs": 356,
    "toolCount": 0,
    "toolNames": []
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: final-head embedded native subagent runs enforce `toolsAllow` before the request reaches the model transport, and explicit `[]` disables tools instead of falling back to unrestricted defaults. CLI-backed native runs fail closed instead of silently ignoring the allowlist.
- Before evidence: without this PR, `sessions_spawn` had no `toolsAllow` schema entry and the spawned child Gateway `agent` request could not carry a per-spawn allowlist.
- What was not tested: a live remote model-backed spawned child run. The proof intentionally uses a mock OpenAI server to avoid real provider calls while still exercising the Gateway and embedded-runner path.

## Supplemental local timing note
The proof run also recorded local mock wall time: baseline `2296ms`, allowlisted `440ms`, empty allowlist `356ms`. This is included only as a local mock timing signal from one isolated run, not as a formal benchmark or guaranteed provider/model speedup. The primary value of this PR is correctness/control-plane narrowing; any latency win depends on tool set, runtime config, and provider behavior.

## Root Cause (if applicable)
N/A. This is additive feature plumbing over an existing embedded-runner allowlist mechanism.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
- Target test or file: `src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts`, `src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts`, `src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts`
- Scenario the tests lock in: `toolsAllow` is exposed, normalized, validates string arrays, preserves explicit `[]`, rejects ACP runtime usage, forwards through Gateway `agent`, and reaches embedded runs started by the command runner.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: this PR connects an existing embedded-runner option to `sessions_spawn`; focused tests cover the schema, validation, and forwarding contract without requiring live model behavior.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes
`runtime="subagent"` callers may pass `toolsAllow` to narrow the spawned native subagent's tool set. Defaults are unchanged when the field is omitted. `toolsAllow: []` intentionally runs the child without tools. `runtime="acp"` rejects the option with a clear input error.

## Diagram (if applicable)
N/A.

## Security Impact (required)
- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: this only narrows the child tool set for native subagent runs. ACP rejects the option because it does not use this embedded tool construction path.

## Repro + Verification
### Environment
- OS: macOS 26.2
- Runtime/container: Node v25.9.0, pnpm 10.33.2, local source checkout
- Model/provider: mock OpenAI `/v1/responses`; no live remote model call
- Integration/channel: local loopback Gateway `agent` RPC and `agent.wait`
- Relevant config: temp workspace, temp state dir, token-auth loopback Gateway, mock provider `mock-openai/gpt-pr2-tools`

### Commands and checks
- `pnpm test src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts` passed (`5 files`, `186 tests`, 2 Vitest shards).
- `pnpm tsgo:core` passed.
- `pnpm lint:core` passed (`0 warnings and 0 errors`).
- `git diff --check` passed before the docs/changelog commit.
- `git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD` passed after the CLI fail-closed review-fix commit.
- `pnpm prompt:snapshots:check` printed `Prompt snapshots are current (7 files)`; the process then hung after printing and was killed.
- `pnpm build` passed before the current-head Gateway/mock proof.
- Final-head Gateway/mock proof above passed on `09471b37572d88702c57190bc9c0cdfccc303385`.

### Expected
- Omitted `toolsAllow`: default native child tool set is unchanged.
- `toolsAllow: ["read", "exec"]`: the child model request only sees `read` and `exec` tools.
- `toolsAllow: []`: the child model request sees no tools.

### Actual
- Omitted `toolsAllow`: 21 tools captured by mock OpenAI.
- `toolsAllow: ["read", "exec"]`: 2 tools captured by mock OpenAI, exactly `exec` and `read`.
- `toolsAllow: []`: 0 tools captured by mock OpenAI.

## Evidence
- [x] Gateway-backed terminal capture
- [x] Mock transport request capture
- [x] Focused tests, typecheck, lint, whitespace check

## Human Verification (required)
- Verified scenarios: schema exposure, native forwarding path, Gateway forwarding path, embedded command runner propagation, strict validation tests, ACP rejection tests, CLI-backed native fail-closed behavior for non-empty and empty allowlists, embedded empty-allowlist minimal prompt behavior, formatter/typecheck/lint/whitespace checks, final-head Gateway behavior proof.
- Edge cases checked: omitted `toolsAllow`, explicit empty `[]`, blank strings trimmed/filtered, non-string entries rejected, non-array input rejected, ACP runtime rejected, CLI-backed native runtime rejected.
- What you did **not** verify: a live provider-backed spawned child run.

## Review Conversations
- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration
- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations
- Risk: callers may expect `toolsAllow` to affect ACP runtime spawns.
  - Mitigation: the tool rejects `toolsAllow` for `runtime="acp"` with a clear input error.
- Risk: callers may expect `toolsAllow` to affect CLI-backed native runs.
  - Mitigation: CLI-backed native runs reject `toolsAllow` before dispatch because they cannot enforce the embedded-runner allowlist.
- Risk: public API naming/semantics need maintainer judgment.
  - Mitigation: the implementation is additive and reuses the existing embedded-runner allowlist semantics rather than adding a second filtering mechanism.

target.title: fix(commitments): stop hardcoding fastMode in extraction (#78451)
target.body:
## Summary

MiniMax Coding Plan operators whose plan owns `MiniMax-M2.7` but not the `-highspeed` variant hit `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` on every commitment extraction pass, because the commitments extraction path passed `fastMode: true` unconditionally to `runEmbeddedPiAgent`, which caused the MiniMax stream wrapper's `createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` to rewrite the base model id to `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`.

## Root Cause

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts:242` (pre-fix): the call to `runEmbeddedPiAgent` for hidden commitment extraction hardcoded `fastMode: true`.
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24`: the MiniMax stream wrapper accepts a `fastMode` boolean and, when true, swaps the base model id to its `-highspeed` variant.
- Result: every commitments extraction request silently flipped `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, which Coding Plan operators do not own. MiniMax responds with HTTP 500 / error code 2061. Reporter verified the Coding Plan owns the base model but not highspeed.
- The reporter listed three valid fix shapes (drop the hardcode, add a config field, or inherit agent fastMode). This PR uses option 1: dropping the hardcode lets the existing `resolveFastModeState` helper continue to control fast-mode for the rest of the agent runtime, and commitments extraction inherits the same default-false behavior.

## Changes

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts`: remove `fastMode: true` from the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload built in the commitments extraction path. Add a comment explaining the reason so this does not regress.
- `src/commitments/runtime.test.ts`: extend the existing `runEmbeddedPiAgentMock` assertion to assert `fastMode` is **not** in the payload, locking in the regression fix.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: single-line entry under Unreleased / Fixes.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: On every commitment extraction pass, MiniMax Coding Plan operators receive `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` from the MiniMax server because the agent runtime requested `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed` even though their plan only owns `MiniMax-M2.7`. The reporter (`@advaitpaliwal`) verified `commitments.enabled: true, commitments.maxPerDay: 10` config, then ran a real Coding Plan session, observed extraction always selecting the highspeed variant, and traced the root cause to `dist/runtime-CXt7BFiH.js` line 386 (`fastMode: true` hardcoded). Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78451
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-78451` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. The smoke script reads the **patched production file `src/commitments/runtime.ts` directly from the worktree**, locates the entire `defaultExtractBatch` function (the path that builds the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload), prints its SHA-256 + verbatim bytes, then runs structural assertions over the captured payload literal to prove `fastMode: true` is gone, the `#78451` explanatory comment is in place, and every sibling field is preserved. No mocks, no test runner — the patched source bytes drive the smoke. PR head: `a3189f329687fa8c2c9da783afbc0d644ea94573`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `git checkout fix/commitments-no-fastmode-hardcode && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts`, where `smoke-pr78459.mts` reads `src/commitments/runtime.ts`, slices the `async function defaultExtractBatch` body, hashes it, prints the verbatim payload object literal, and runs structural assertions against the live patched source.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output captured locally on PR head `a3189f3296` (Windows 11 + Node 22.14, the patched `src/commitments/runtime.ts` `defaultExtractBatch` function read from the worktree, 1660 bytes, SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379`).

~~~text
$ node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts
=== Patched defaultExtractBatch function — bytes from src/commitments/runtime.ts ===
offset: 7245 -> 8905 ( 1660 bytes)
SHA-256: ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379

function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false
explanatory '#78451' comment present                : true
  preserved field 'disableTools: true': true
  preserved field 'thinkLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'verboseLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'reasoningLevel: "off"': true

=== Payload object passed to runEmbeddedPiAgent (verbatim from patched code) ===
{ cfg, agentId: first.agentId });
  const result = await runEmbeddedPiAgent({
    sessionId: runId,
    sessionKey: `agent:${first.agentId}:commitments:${runId}`,
    agentId: first.agentId,
    trigger: "manual",
    sessionFile: resolveExtractionSessionFile(first.agentId, runId),
    workspaceDir: resolveAgentWorkspaceDir(cfg, first.agentId),
    config: cfg,
    provider: modelRef.provider,
    model: modelRef.model,
    prompt: buildCommitmentExtractionPrompt({ cfg, items: params.items }),
    disableTools: true,
    thinkLevel: "off",
    verboseLevel: "off",
    reasoningLevel: "off",
    // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
    // commitments extraction inherit the configured fast-mode state (which
    // defaults to false). Hardcoding true caused MiniMax Coding Plans without
    // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
    // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
    timeoutMs: resolved.extraction.timeoutSeconds * 1000,
    runId,
    bootstrapContextMode: "lightweight",
    skillsSnapshot: { prompt: "", skills: [] },
    suppressToolErrorWarnings: true,
  }

=== fastMode-related lines in the payload (BEFORE-vs-AFTER comparison) ===
  // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
  // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
  // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
~~~

- **Observed result after fix**: The patched `defaultExtractBatch` function — pinned by a 1660-byte / SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379` integrity check — has zero `fastMode: true` literals (`function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false`). The explanatory `#78451` comment is in place (`explanatory '#78451' comment present: true`), and every sibling field (`disableTools: true`, `thinkLevel: "off"`, `verboseLevel: "off"`, `reasoningLevel: "off"`) is preserved verbatim. The captured payload literal shown verbatim above confirms `runEmbeddedPiAgent` is now called without a `fastMode` key — so the MiniMax stream wrapper at `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24` no longer flips `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, and the operator's configured base model id flows through unchanged.
- **What was not tested**: A live HTTP roundtrip against the real MiniMax server with a Coding Plan token is not available on the contributor machine, so this is source-level / structural proof against the patched payload-builder, not a live MiniMax 200 response. Maintainers may apply `proof: override` if a live MiniMax recording is required; the reporter's original issue already contains live HTTP 500 evidence from the real Coding Plan.

## Test

- `pnpm test src/commitments/runtime.test.ts` — 6 passed (including the new `expect(callArgs).not.toHaveProperty("fastMode")` regression assertion).

## Notes

- Scope: smallest complete fix. No new config surface and no provider/wrapper edits — the existing fast-mode resolution chain already covers the right behavior; the hardcode was the only thing forcing `true`.
- Compatibility: agents that need fast-mode for commitments can still set it via `agents.defaults.models.<id>.params.fastMode: true` (or per-agent `fastModeDefault: true`) — the `resolveFastModeState` chain is unchanged.

Closes #78451

Reproducibility: yes. The high-confidence path is a fake MCP server that connects, then delays or hangs `tools/list`; current main has no OpenClaw list-timeout option, and the PR body supplies after-fix terminal output for both default and explicit-timeout behavior.
